title: Dialectal Filtering
subtitle: Synthesising dialect-specific corpora from large noisy text collections

sections.heading: Overview
sections.value: Kurdish NLP faces a fundamental data problem: available text corpora are large but extremely noisy, mixing multiple dialects, scripts, and registers without labelling. A model trained on such data learns an undifferentiated blend rather than the properties of any specific variety. Dialectal Filtering addresses this by using lexicographic features - dialect-specific markers identified from linguistic resources - to filter and partition large raw corpora into variety-specific sub-corpora.

sections.heading: The Core Idea
sections.value: The approach treats dialect identification as a lightweight, lexicon-driven filtering step rather than a full classification task. For each target dialect, a set of discriminative lexical and morphological markers is identified. Documents containing a sufficient density of these markers are assigned to the corresponding dialect sub-corpus. This keeps the pipeline fast, interpretable, and applicable even when only small amounts of labelled data are available.

sections.heading: Results
items: Over 100 dialect-specific sub-corpora synthesised from Northern Kurdish (Kurmanji) source data.
Presented at the 7th International Conference on Kurdish Linguistics (ICKL-7, Bamberg 2025).
Current limitations in precision are acknowledged; further refinement of the marker lexicon is ongoing.
[TODO: add quantitative results once finalised for publication]

sections.heading: Connection to the Broader PhD Project
sections.value: Dialectal Filtering is one of the core data-creation threads of the Dialect-Aware NLP PhD project. The sub-corpora it produces feed directly into downstream tasks such as dialect-aware machine translation and ASR fine-tuning (see: Kurdish Voices).

sections.heading: Next Steps
items: Expand the marker lexicon to cover additional Kurdish varieties beyond Northern Kurdish.
Evaluate downstream task performance (MT, ASR) on the synthesised sub-corpora.
Investigate combining the lexicon-based approach with lightweight classification models.
